choices.text: Workers deserve the full value of what they produce and should collectively own the means of production
choices.explanation: This describes Marx's labor theory of value and socialist economics — Smith believed in private ownership and markets, not collective worker ownership.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Industrial Revolution on economic thought.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "By 1900, the United States had overtaken Britain as the world's largest industrial economy. American industrialization was characterized by large-scale corporate organization, mass production techniques (later Fordism), and a vast domestic market protected by high tariffs — differing significantly from British free-trade industrialization." — Adapted from an economic history

questions.question: The comparison in the passage best illustrates which historical pattern regarding industrialization?

choices.text: Industrialization spread globally but took different institutional forms in different national contexts
choices.explanation: The passage contrasts British free-trade industrialization with American tariff-protected corporate capitalism — demonstrating that industrialization was not a single model but adapted to different national circumstances, institutions, and political choices.

choices.text: American industrialization exactly replicated the British model, confirming industrialization had only one pathway
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly contrasts American (large corporations, high tariffs, mass production) with British (free trade) industrialization — highlighting their differences, not their identity.

choices.text: Britain deliberately slowed American industrialization to maintain its economic dominance
choices.explanation: Britain did initially try to prevent industrial technology from leaving (prohibiting emigration of skilled workers, export of machinery) but could not sustain this policy. American industrialization proceeded despite British preferences.

choices.text: Only nations with extensive colonial empires could successfully industrialize
choices.explanation: The United States industrialized successfully with minimal overseas colonies — its domestic market and continental resources drove industrialization. The colonial empire hypothesis is challenged by American industrialization.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Industrial Revolution.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the 1848 revolutions in Europe ultimately failed to achieve their political goals?

choices.text: Divisions between liberal nationalists seeking constitutions and radical workers seeking social reform allowed conservative forces to isolate and suppress each group separately
choices.explanation: The 1848 revolutions collapsed because middle-class liberals feared worker radicalism more than they wanted constitutional government — they supported conservative suppression of workers rather than maintaining the revolutionary coalition. Conservative forces exploited these divisions.

choices.text: The revolutionary movements lacked any organization or political leadership
choices.explanation: 1848 revolutions had significant leadership — the Frankfurt Parliament in Germany, Lamartine in France, Mazzini in Italy. The failure was not lack of leadership but political divisions and conservative military force.

choices.text: The Catholic Church successfully united all European conservatives against revolutionary movements through papal decree
choices.explanation: While Pope Pius IX initially sympathized with liberal nationalism before turning conservative, the Church's role was not the primary reason revolutions failed. Military forces loyal to traditional monarchies were more decisive.

choices.text: Economic prosperity in 1848 reduced popular grievances and eliminated the social conditions for revolution
choices.explanation: 1848 was preceded by the 'hungry 1840s' — economic depression, crop failures (including the Irish Famine), and industrial unemployment. Economic hardship contributed to the revolutions — prosperity was not the reason they failed.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of political revolutions.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: The Declaration of Independence (1776) and Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789) both drew most directly on which intellectual tradition?

choices.text: The European Enlightenment's philosophy of natural rights, popular sovereignty, and social contract
choices.explanation: Both documents reflect core Enlightenment ideas: Locke's natural rights (life, liberty, property/happiness), Rousseau's popular sovereignty, and Montesquieu's constitutionalism. The Enlightenment was the common intellectual source for both Atlantic revolutions.

choices.text: Medieval European Christian theology and the divine right of kings
choices.explanation: Both declarations explicitly challenged divine right monarchy — the American by establishing a republic, the French by placing sovereignty in 'the nation.' These documents represent a break from, not a continuation of, medieval Christian political theology.

choices.text: Ancient Greek democracy as practiced in Athens during the 5th century BCE
choices.explanation: While Enlightenment thinkers were aware of classical precedents, the immediate intellectual source for these declarations was 17th-18th century Enlightenment philosophy. Direct invocation of Athenian democracy was not their framework.

choices.text: Islamic political philosophy from the Ottoman Empire's legal traditions
choices.explanation: Both documents drew on Western European Enlightenment philosophy. While intercultural influence exists historically, Islamic political philosophy was not the immediate intellectual source for American or French revolutionary documents.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of Enlightenment political thought.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Russian Revolution of 1905 revealed the weakness of the Tsarist state: the military was exhausted from the disastrous Russo-Japanese War, workers were striking across industrial cities, and peasants were seizing noble land. Tsar Nicholas II issued the October Manifesto promising a constitution and legislature — but reversed these promises when the crisis passed." — Adapted from a history of Russia

questions.question: The 1905 Revolution's outcome best illustrates which historical pattern?

choices.text: Authoritarian regimes can survive revolutionary crises by making temporary concessions they later reverse when their power is restored
choices.explanation: Nicholas II's October Manifesto illustrates the classic pattern of authoritarian concession under pressure followed by reversal — the Duma (parliament) was created but systematically undermined after the revolution subsided. This pattern of reform reversal fueled the 1917 revolution.

choices.text: Revolutionary movements always succeed when workers and peasants unite against an autocratic government
choices.explanation: The 1905 revolution failed despite significant worker and peasant mobilization — the government survived by making temporary concessions and using military force. Revolutionary success is not guaranteed by social mobilization alone.

choices.text: Constitutional reforms immediately stabilized Russia's political system and prevented further revolutionary unrest
choices.explanation: The 1905 concessions were insufficient and reversed — leading directly to greater revolutionary radicalization. Russia experienced full revolution in 1917, demonstrating that the 1905 reforms did not stabilize the political system.

choices.text: Military defeat automatically caused the collapse of all authoritarian regimes in the early 20th century
choices.explanation: Military defeat weakened but did not automatically collapse the Russian state in 1905 — the regime survived another 12 years. Military defeat was a contributing factor to revolutionary crisis, not an automatic cause of regime collapse.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how states responded to political challenges.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following most accurately describes the role of the Enlightenment in the Atlantic Revolutions of the 18th and early 19th centuries?

choices.text: Enlightenment ideas provided the intellectual framework justifying revolution, but economic and social grievances provided the actual motivations for revolutionary action
choices.explanation: Ideas alone do not make revolutions — the American colonists had real economic grievances (taxation without representation, trade restrictions), France had fiscal crisis and hunger, Haiti had the lived reality of slavery. Enlightenment ideas gave these grievances a legitimizing framework.

choices.text: Enlightenment philosophers directly organized and led revolutionary armies in the field
choices.explanation: Enlightenment philosophers (most were dead before the major revolutions) did not lead armies. Revolutionary leaders (Washington, Robespierre, Toussaint Louverture, Bolívar) drew on Enlightenment ideas but were not themselves primarily philosophers.

choices.text: Enlightenment ideas were irrelevant to the revolutions, which were driven purely by economic competition between nations
choices.explanation: Revolutionary leaders explicitly invoked Enlightenment concepts — natural rights, popular sovereignty, social contract. The Declaration of Independence and Declaration of the Rights of Man directly translate Enlightenment philosophy into political documents.

choices.text: Enlightenment ideas spread only within Europe and had no influence on revolutions in the Americas
choices.explanation: Enlightenment ideas spread across the Atlantic — American founders read Locke and Montesquieu; Haitian revolutionary leaders were familiar with French Enlightenment thought; Latin American creoles studied Rousseau. Atlantic circulation of ideas was central to the revolutionary age.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of Enlightenment political thought.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Taiping Heavenly Kingdom (1850–1864) was simultaneously China's deadliest civil war and a remarkable social experiment. Its leader, Hong Xiuquan, claimed to be the younger brother of Jesus Christ and established a theocratic kingdom in southern China that banned opium and prostitution, redistributed land, and gave women official roles — before being crushed by Qing forces aided by Western officers." — Adapted from a history of 19th-century China

questions.question: The Taiping Rebellion is most significant as evidence of which historical development?

choices.text: The syncretism of Western Christianity with Chinese religious and political traditions producing radical indigenous social movements
choices.explanation: Hong Xiuquan's hybrid ideology — combining Christian millenarianism with Chinese traditions and radical social reforms — illustrates how Western cultural contact could produce distinctly Chinese revolutionary movements, rather than simple adoption of Western ideas.

choices.text: The successful democratization of China through a peasant-led constitutional revolution
choices.explanation: The Taiping Kingdom was a theocracy (rule by Hong as divine king), not a democracy. It was crushed, not successful. Democratic constitutional reform was not its outcome.

choices.text: The complete rejection of all foreign ideas by Chinese peasants who supported the Qing Dynasty
choices.explanation: The Taiping movement embraced (transformed) Christian ideas — it was not a rejection of foreign influence. The rebels opposed the Qing Dynasty; it was the Qing who received Western military assistance (Ever Victorious Army) in crushing the rebellion.

choices.text: Western Christian missionaries' success in converting the Chinese elite to orthodox Christianity by 1850
choices.explanation: Chinese elite conversion to orthodox Christianity was minimal. Hong Xiuquan's version of Christianity was considered heretical by Western missionaries. The Taiping movement reflects a heterodox synthesis, not mainstream missionary success.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how contact with Western powers transformed non-European societies.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: How did the role of religion in public life change as a result of the French Revolution?

choices.text: The Revolution promoted secularism by attacking Church authority, seizing Church property, and briefly de-Christianizing France — establishing a model of separation between church and state
choices.explanation: The Revolution confiscated Church lands (1789), abolished monasteries, required clergy to swear loyalty to the state, briefly established a 'Cult of Reason' and then 'Cult of the Supreme Being' replacing Christianity. The Napoleonic Concordat (1801) re-established the Church but as subordinate to state — a secularizing legacy.

choices.text: The Revolution strengthened the Catholic Church by making it the official religion of the French state with expanded powers
choices.explanation: The Revolution attacked and weakened the Catholic Church — confiscating property, requiring loyalty oaths, and briefly attempting to replace Christianity. Napoleon's Concordat restored a subordinate role for the Church, not an expanded one.

choices.text: The Revolution had no impact on the relationship between religion and French public life
choices.explanation: The Revolution fundamentally transformed church-state relations in France — from the confiscation of Church lands in 1789 to Napoleon's Concordat to the eventual full secularism (laïcité) of the Third Republic. The Revolution initiated France's distinctive secular tradition.

choices.text: The Revolution converted France from Catholicism to Protestantism as part of its reform agenda
choices.explanation: The Revolution did not convert France to Protestantism. Its attack on religion was secular in nature — replacing Christian worship with civic cults of reason and virtue before Napoleon restored a diminished Catholic role.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the effects of the French Revolution.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "By 1900, the global economy was more integrated than at any previous time: a network of submarine telegraph cables connected continents in real time; steam-powered ships crossed the Atlantic in days; railway networks tied continental interiors to coastal ports. A commodity price change in Chicago could affect farmers in Argentina within hours." — Adapted from a history of globalization

questions.question: The developments described in the passage most directly resulted from which combination of factors?

choices.text: Industrial technologies (telegraph, steam power, railways) that drastically reduced the cost and time of communication and transportation across global distances
choices.explanation: The passage specifically identifies submarine telegraph cables, steam ships, and railways as the technologies creating near-real-time global economic integration — all products of industrial technological innovation in the 19th century.

choices.text: International political agreements that eliminated all tariffs and allowed completely free global trade
choices.explanation: While free trade agreements expanded (Cobden-Chevalier Treaty 1860), many nations (especially the US) maintained high protective tariffs. Global integration was driven primarily by technology, not universal free trade agreements.

choices.text: The establishment of a single world government that coordinated global economic policy
choices.explanation: No world government existed in 1900. Global integration was achieved through market forces and technological change, not political unification under a central authority.

choices.text: Cultural homogenization that made all nations adopt identical economic practices simultaneously
choices.explanation: Economic practices varied significantly — Britain operated free trade while the US had high tariffs; Germany pursued state-supported industrialization. Integration occurred despite different national economic models, not through cultural homogenization.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the effects of the Industrial Revolution on global commerce.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains the long-term political impact of the Haitian Revolution on the wider world?

choices.text: It terrified slaveholders across the Americas while inspiring enslaved people and abolitionists, accelerating the debate over slavery and demonstrating that enslaved people could achieve freedom by force
choices.explanation: The Haitian Revolution simultaneously terrified slaveholders (who restricted slave literacy and imposed new controls) while inspiring enslaved people (Gabriel Prosser, Denmark Vesey, Nat Turner in the US all invoked Haiti) and abolitionists who used it as evidence that slavery would produce violent resistance.

choices.text: It led to the immediate abolition of slavery throughout the Americas as other nations followed Haiti's example
choices.explanation: Slavery continued in the Americas for decades after 1804 — Cuba and Brazil until the 1880s, the US until 1865. Haiti's revolution actually caused many slaveholders to tighten controls, fearing similar uprisings.

choices.text: It caused France to adopt a democratic constitution and permanently abolish slavery in all territories
choices.explanation: Napoleon had already reinstated slavery in French colonies in 1802 (just before Haitian independence) and maintained it in remaining French colonies. France did not permanently abolish colonial slavery until 1848.

choices.text: It had no significant impact outside of Haiti because the revolution was too geographically isolated to influence other regions
choices.explanation: Haiti's revolution had significant global impact — inspiring Black freedom struggles, influencing the US Louisiana Purchase (Napoleon sold Louisiana after losing Haiti), and terrifying slaveholder societies across the Americas.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of the Haitian Revolution.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The leaders of Latin American independence were overwhelmingly creole men of property who feared social revolution from below — from enslaved people, indigenous communities, and mixed-race populations — at least as much as they resented colonial restrictions from above. Independence preserved rather than dismantled most colonial social hierarchies." — Adapted from a history of Latin American independence

questions.question: The historian's argument most directly challenges which popular narrative about Latin American independence?

choices.text: That Latin American independence represented a broad social revolution that transformed the lives of all colonial subjects
choices.explanation: The historian argues independence was primarily a political transfer of power among elites — colonial social hierarchies (racial, economic, gender) were preserved. For most indigenous peoples, enslaved Africans, and the poor, independence changed little in their daily lives.

choices.text: That independence was achieved primarily through military means rather than peaceful negotiation
choices.explanation: The passage focuses on the social conservatism of independence leaders, not on military vs. diplomatic methods. The passage does not challenge the military nature of independence movements.

choices.text: That Enlightenment ideas played any role in inspiring Latin American independence leaders
choices.explanation: The passage does not deny Enlightenment influence — it acknowledges creole leaders read Enlightenment philosophy. The argument is that their social conservatism constrained how far they applied those ideas.

choices.text: That Spain was an economically exploitative colonial power that restricted creole economic opportunities
choices.explanation: The passage acknowledges creoles resented 'colonial restrictions from above' — it does not deny Spanish economic exploitation. The argument is about what independence did (and didn't) change for non-elite populations.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of Latin American independence movements.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best describes Charles Darwin's significance for 19th-century thought beyond natural science?

choices.text: His theory of natural selection was misapplied to justify Social Darwinism — the belief that human societies and races were subject to competitive selection, legitimizing imperialism and social inequality
choices.explanation: Darwin's evolutionary biology was extrapolated into 'Social Darwinism' by Herbert Spencer and others, who applied 'survival of the fittest' to human races and nations — providing pseudo-scientific justification for imperialism ('civilized' races dominating 'inferior' ones) and economic inequality.

choices.text: Darwin's work had no influence outside biology and was completely ignored by social scientists and politicians
choices.explanation: Darwinian ideas profoundly influenced 19th-century social thought, imperialist ideology, nationalist theory, and economics. 'Social Darwinism' was among the most influential ideological currents of the late 19th century.

choices.text: Darwin's work immediately reconciled science and religion, eliminating all conflict between evolutionary biology and Christian theology
choices.explanation: Darwin's theory caused profound religious controversy that has never been fully resolved — it challenged literal interpretations of creation narratives. 'Reconciliation' was not the immediate or universal response.

choices.text: Darwin argued that human races were separate species created separately by God, justifying racial hierarchy
choices.explanation: Darwin argued the opposite — that all humans shared common ancestry (monogenism). It was misinterpreters of Darwin (Social Darwinists) who used evolutionary language to justify racial hierarchy, not Darwin himself, who opposed polygenist racism.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the effects of the Industrial Revolution on intellectual thought.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following most accurately compares the outcomes of the Meiji Restoration (Japan) and the Self-Strengthening Movement (China) by 1900?

choices.text: Japan industrialized successfully and became a regional military power capable of defeating China (1895) and Russia (1905), while China remained relatively weak and was carved into spheres of influence by Western powers
choices.explanation: Japan's comprehensive institutional reform (new constitution, modern military, industrial development, universal education) enabled rapid modernization. China's more conservative Self-Strengthening Movement, which avoided political reform, left it vulnerable — defeated by Japan in 1895 and carved into spheres of influence by European powers and Japan.

choices.text: Both Japan and China successfully resisted Western imperialism and remained fully sovereign by 1900
choices.explanation: China was carved into spheres of influence and suffered the Boxer Rebellion and subsequent occupation. Japan succeeded in maintaining sovereignty, but China did not — their outcomes differed dramatically.

choices.text: China industrialized faster than Japan because of its larger population and greater natural resources
choices.explanation: Japan industrialized significantly faster and more successfully than China in the 19th century. Population size and natural resources did not compensate for China's lack of political institutional reform.

choices.text: Both nations adopted identical democratic constitutional systems by 1900 in response to Western pressure
choices.explanation: Japan adopted a constitutional monarchy (Meiji Constitution, 1889) with limited democratic features. China had no constitution until after the Qing Dynasty fell in 1912. Neither adopted identical nor fully democratic systems.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how states outside Europe responded to Western power.

questions.subject: ap-world-history
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the Industrial Revolution did NOT develop in India, despite India's large population and textile traditions?

choices.text: British colonial policy actively deindustrialized India by destroying Indian textile industries to protect British manufactures and kept India as a raw material supplier rather than allowing industrial development
choices.explanation: Britain imposed tariffs on Indian textiles entering Britain while flooding India with cheap British machine-made cloth, destroying India's famous cottage textile industry. Colonial policy deliberately maintained India as a raw material supplier (cotton, indigo) and market for British manufactured goods.

choices.text: Indian culture rejected all technology and innovation on religious grounds
choices.explanation: India had sophisticated craft traditions and technological knowledge. The barrier to industrialization was political (colonial extraction and deindustrialization) and economic (colonial trade policies), not cultural rejection of technology.

choices.text: India lacked raw materials needed for industrialization, particularly coal and iron
choices.explanation: India had substantial coal and iron reserves — in fact, Tata Steel was founded in 1907 and became one of Asia's largest steel companies. Resource availability was not the primary barrier.

choices.text: Indian rulers chose to invest in agriculture rather than industry because farming was more profitable
choices.explanation: Under British rule, Indian rulers had limited economic sovereignty. Colonial revenue extraction (the 'drain of wealth') and trade policies limited investment in industrialization — it was not simply a choice by Indian rulers.

questions.unit_objective: Explain the causes and effects of industrialization on non-European regions.

questions.subject: ap-world-history

stimulus.content: "The Chartist movement in Britain (1838–1857) demanded: universal male suffrage, secret ballot, equal electoral districts, payment for Members of Parliament, abolition of property qualifications for MPs, and annual elections. None of these demands was met during the movement's active years — yet all were eventually enacted over the following century." — Adapted from a history of British politics

questions.question: The Chartist movement's trajectory best illustrates which historical pattern?

choices.text: Unsuccessful reform movements can establish demands that become the basis for gradual political change achieved over longer time horizons
choices.explanation: Chartism's failure to achieve immediate goals did not mean it had no lasting impact — its demands became the platform for subsequent reform. By 1918, all Chartist demands except annual elections had been implemented, showing how reform movements can define agendas that eventually succeed despite initial defeat.

choices.text: Working-class political movements never achieve their goals and have no lasting historical significance
choices.explanation: The passage explicitly shows Chartist demands were eventually enacted — they did achieve their goals, just not immediately. Working-class political movements have had significant lasting impact on democratic development.

choices.text: The British government's immediate embrace of democratic reform prevented revolutionary violence
choices.explanation: The British government did NOT immediately embrace Chartist demands — it rejected them repeatedly. The passage notes none were met during the movement's active years. Gradual (not immediate) reform characterized Britain's democratic development.

choices.text: Democratic political change requires violent revolution rather than peaceful political organizing
choices.explanation: Chartism was primarily a peaceful political movement (though with some violent moments). Britain achieved major democratic reform without full-scale revolution — demonstrating that peaceful organizing could eventually produce change, even if slowly.

questions.unit_objective: Explain how working-class movements responded to industrialization.
